MTH6139 Time Series CW1

Coursework 1 – 231173052

Elliott Stoute

Spring term 2026

Section 0: Overview

This is the MTH6139 Time Series Coursework 1 based on time series data of Australian Beer production from 1990 to 1995. I have done this using Meta’s Prophet R package.Prophet is a forecasting package typically used for time series data.Data is in Mega Litres.

Section 1: R Code

I ran “install.packages(”remotes”)” and “remotes::install_github(‘facebook/prophet@*release’, subdir=‘R’)” in the console first. Hence I have iused the latest version of Meta’s Prophet.

## Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0.
## ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`.

## generated.

Section 1.1 Explanation

I start out by loading the Meta Prophet package in R using the most updated version. Initially the data goes back to January 1956 but this was too large of a data set so I sub set the data to cover 1990-1995 (the last 5 years of the data). Then I had to edit the CSV file in excel to change the date label to ds and production header to y to fit the prophet format. And appending the date to then be YYYY-MM-DD by adding “-01” to the ds column which added the “-DD”.

Moving onto the use of Prophet, I use the prophet fucntion from the prophet library based on the AusBeerProd data with only Yearly seasonality being held TRUE. Use of additive seasonality as seasonality between trend cycle is consistent over the 5 years.

FutureDates makes a data frame that adds 36 periods which is 36 months worth of data points to the beer data. The Predict function then uses such points and the fitted model to forecast future results.

I then plot the forecast data and its components to compare seasonality.

Section 2: Plots

Section 2.1 Meta Prophet Plots

plot(AusBeerProdModel, AusBeerProdForecast)

prophet::prophet_plot_components(AusBeerProdModel, AusBeerProdForecast)

Section 2.2 Raw Data Plot

#Plot of raw data
plot(AusBeerProd,type = "l", xlab ="Date", ylab="Beer Production in ML", main = "Australian Beer Production 1990-1995 (Monthly)")

Section 2.3 Explanation of Plots

Plot 1: Plots the raw data points(Black Dots), Fitted Model & Forecast (Solid Blue Line) and Uncertainty area (Shaded Blue). There are only 5 raw data points that sit outside the uncertainty between Jan 1990 and Jan 1995 (61 data points). We see peaks at the May & Sep-Nov period and the forecast continues this trend with overall decline in peak values over the full 8 year term.

Plot 2: Plots the trend and yearly seasonality. The overall trend is a steady long term decline in beer production likely caused by consumer taste changes and economic factors such as a recession. In terms of yearly seasonality peaks at May & Sep-Nov which marks autumn into winter seasons and spring into summer respectively. As expected beer consumption would increase in a summer period as weather heats up and in periods of poorer weather as more people gather indoors together.

Plot 3: The raw data shows strong seasonality at the end of each year in the summer/holiday period of Christmas and New Years. As well as a steady decline in peak volume produced monthly year-on-year.
